coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-decorated porous organic cage as fluorescent sensor for rapid discrimination of nitrophenol isomers and chiral alcohols
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhenyu
Lu, Xiaotian
Zhong, Yanhui
Hu, Yufei
Li, Gongke
Zhang, Runkun
coredata.dc:description: Isomers discrimination plays a vital role in modern chemistry, and development of efficient and rapid method to achieve this aim has attracted a great deal of interest. In this work, a novel carbon dot-decorated chiral porous organic cage hybrid nanocomposite (CD@RCC3) was prepared and used to fabricate fluorescent sensor. The resultant CD@RCC3 was characterized by using a range of techniques, finding that CD@RCC3 possesses strong and stable fluorescent property in common organic solvents, especially it exhibits chiral property. The potential application of CD@RCC3 in fluorescence sensing was demonstrated by isomers discrimination. The designed sensor was successfully used to rapid discriminate nitrophenol isomers. Meanwhile, it exhibited differentiation ability towards phenylalaninol and phenylethanol enantiomers. Our work enriches the type of synthetic materials for fluorescence sensing, and provides a simple method for distinguishing structural isomers and chiral isomers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous organic cage
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Molecular recognition
Chiral discrimination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General procedures for fluorescence sensing experiments
General procedures for lifetime decay experiments

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra of CDs (1), CD-COCl (2), RCC3 (3), CD@RCC3 (4); (b) TGA analyses under an inert atmosphere of CDs (1), CD@RCC3 (2), RCC3 (3); (c–e) XPS survey, and deconvoluted spectra of CD@RCC3 (1) and CDs (2): C 1s spectra and O 1s; (f) N 1s spectra of CD@RCC3.
(a) TEM image of CD@RCC3 (inset: size histogram); (b) AFM image of the as-prepared CD@RCC3.

(a) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs in methanol solution at different excitation wavelengths. (b) UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of CD@RCC3 in methanol solution at different excitation wavelengths. The inset shows the photographs of the CDs and CD@RCC3 solutions under UV light illumination.
(a) Fluorescencet spectra of CD@RCC3 solution (1) with MNP (2), ONP (3) and PNP (4) at concentrations of 30 mM; (b) The quenching plots of ONP, MNP and PNP with CD@RCC3.

(a–b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD@RCC3 upon titration with increasing volume of D-/L-PA: 0, 11, 22, 42, 62, 82, 100 μM (λex = 352 nm); (c) Stern-Volmer plots of CD@RCC3 with L-PA (1) and D-PA (2); (d–e) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD@RCC3 upon titration with increasing volume of R-/S-PE: 0, 11, 22, 42, 62, 82, 100 μM (λex = 352 nm); (f) Stern-Volmer plots of CD@RCC3 with S-PE (1) and R-PE (2).

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-decorated porous organic cage as fluorescent sensor for rapid discrimination of nitrophenol isomers and chiral alcohols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou, 510275, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Isomers discrimination plays a vital role in modern chemistry, and development of efficient and rapid method to achieve this aim has attracted a great deal of interest. In this work, a novel carbon dot-decorated chiral porous organic cage hybrid nanocomposite (CD@RCC3) was prepared and used to fabricate fluorescent sensor. The resultant CD@RCC3 was characterized by using a range of techniques, finding that CD@RCC3 possesses strong and stable fluorescent property in common organic solvents, especially it exhibits chiral property. The potential application of CD@RCC3 in fluorescence sensing was demonstrated by isomers discrimination. The designed sensor was successfully used to rapid discriminate nitrophenol isomers. Meanwhile, it exhibited differentiation ability towards phenylalaninol and phenylethanol enantiomers. Our work enriches the type of synthetic materials for fluorescence sensing, and provides a simple method for distinguishing structural isomers and chiral isomers.

ce:section.ce:para: Isomerism is commonly found in organic chemistry, and discrimination between isomers (includes structural and chiral isomers) is becoming one of the most important fields in analytical chemistry due to its important applications. For example, nitrophenol (NP) isomers are fairly important chemical materials for synthetic dyes, pesticides and explosives manufacture. However, they are serious environmental pollutants due to their enormous harm to organisms and environment [1–3]. The toxicity of its isomers is usually different from each other, such as o-nitrophenol (ONP) and p-nitrophenol (PNP) present much more serious impact on the organisms than m-nitrophenol (MNP). Consequently, the discrimination of NP isomers is of great significance for human health and environmental safety. Chirality is an intrinsic property of most biologically important molecules [4,5]. Chiral discrimination plays an important role in chemical, biological and pharmaceutical science due to enantiomers have different pharmacological and biological properties [6,7]. However, it is still a great challenge to isomer analysis due to the similar structure and physical property between isomers.

Gas chromatography [8,9], high-performance liquid chromatography [10], mass spectrometry [11,12], magnetic circular dichroism [13,14] and NMR spectrometry [15] are effective methods for isomer analysis. However, these methods are usually time-consuming, and require sophisticated or expensive instruments. As a consequence, simple, inexpensive, and convenient methods for this purpose are still in demand. Fluorescent sensors have attracted increasing interest because they could provide rapid, low-cost and methods for isomer discrimination. Design of fluorescent materials is very important to chemical sensing, although various materials have been fabricated for fluorescent sensing, the discovery of new sensing materials is still one of the most important factors for the development of this field [16–19]. Porous organic cages (POCs) as a new class of shape-persistent, three-dimensional porous molecules with permanent inherent void space have attracted considerable attention to their potential applications [20–24]. This new type of porous material possesses many intrinsic properties including large surface areas, low framework density and various structural features. Differing from porous materials with frameworks or networks, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs) or conjugated microporous polymers (CMPs), POCs are composed of discrete molecules possessing both intrinsic porosity within the cages and additional extrinsic porosity between the cage units, which results in enormous enhancement in pore volume. In addition, they are easy to dissolve in common organic solvents with solution process ability. These remarkable features make POCs show promising applications to gas adsorption and separation [25,26], gas storage [27,28], catalysis [29–31], molecular recognition [32–35], sensing [36–38], etc. Although POCs have been used as fluorescent chemosensor for nitroaromatics, their application to fluorescent sensor in recognition of chiral compounds has not been reported yet. As one of the most studied prototypical class of POCs, CC3 has been identified as a promising material for molecular recognition because of its good stability, high solubility and unique tetrahedral symmetry, especially the pleasing chiral property. Therefore, CC3 are very suitable to be used as fluorescent chemosensor in molecular recognition. However, CC3 is a non-fluorescent porous molecule, which limits its application in fluorescence field. Post synthetic modification (PSM) of porous molecules can introduce new functional groups into the surface sites or pores, which thereby endows them with new chemical properties so as to expand their application fields. However, limited research articles on PSM of POCs could have been located until now [39].

Our aim is to attach fluorescent groups in chiral framework of RCC3 to offer effective materials for fluorescent discrimination of isomeric compounds. As shown in Fig. 1 , we present a facile route to prepare a new hybrid nanocomposite (CD@RCC3) by PSM of RCC3 with carbon dots (CDs). The as-prepared hybrid nanocomposite was applied to fluorescence sensing of nitrophenol isomers and chiral alcohols. The results showed that CD@RCC3 exhibits favorable differentiation ability towards nitrophenol isomers, phenylalaninol and phenylethanol enantiomers.

(1R, 2R)-1, 2-Diaminocyclohexane (98%, ee: 99%), trifluoroacetic acid (99%), sodium borohydride (98%), citric acid (98%, anhydrous) and thionyl chloride (99%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry (Shanghai, China). 1, 3, 5-triformylbenzene was prepared according to the literature [40]. Dehydrated solvents used throughout this work were obtained from J&K (Beijing, China). All of the other chemical reagents were of analytical grade. All chemicals were used without further purification.

NMR spectra were measured on Bruker Fourier 400 M spectrometer. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) were recorded using a NICOLET AVATAR 330 Fourier transform infrared. TGA analysis was carried out using a TG-209 Thermogravimetry coupled with Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, the samples were heated at the rate of 5 °C/min under N2 atmosphere. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were recorded with a PHILIPS TECNAI 10 TEM instrument (Philips, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed with a PHI Versa Probe spectrometer using Al Kα source (15 Kv, 50W). Fluorescence (FL) spectra was obtained on a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (SHIMADZE, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra was recorded with the UV–vis 2600 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZE, Japan).

The porous organic cage of CC3 crystals were synthesized according to the published procedure of Andrew I. Cooper [41]. Typically, dehydrated dichloromethane (100 mL) was slowly added to solid 1, 3, 5-triformylbenzene (5 g, 30.86 mmol) in a dried 500 mL round-bottom flask without stirring at room temperature. Trifluoroacetic acid (100 μL) was added to this solution as a catalyst for imine bond formation. After 10 min, a solution of (1R, 2R)-1, 2-cyclohexanediamine (5 g, 44.64 mmol) in dichloromethane (100 mL) was added slowly down by the inside wall of flask. The flask was covered and left to stand without shirring at room temperature. After 72 h, white octahedral crystals were obtained by filtration and washed with 95% ethanol/5% dichloromethane. 1H NMR and 13C NMR were used to verified the structure of CC3 (Figs. S1–S2). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz): δ 8.16 (s, 12H, CHN̶), 7.90 (s, 12H, ArH), 3.33 (m, 12H, CHN), 1.82–1.65 (m, 48H, CH2 ) ppm. 13C NMR (CDCl3, 100 MHz): δ 159.19, 136.61, 129.57, 74.63, 33.01, 24.38 ppm.

The porous organic cage of RCC3 crystals were synthesized according to the published procedure of Andrew I. Cooper [42]. The CC3 crystals (0.93 g, 0.83 mmol) were dissolved in 60 mL of a CHCl3/MeOH (1:1 v/v) mixture by stirring. After this mixture solution became clear, NaBH4 (1 g, 26.43 mmol) was added at room temperature, followed by stirring for 12 h. Water (2 mL) was then added, and the reaction stirred for a further 12 h. After completion of the reaction, solvent was completely removed under vacuum, and the product was extracted with CHCl3. The organic phase was washed with water several times and dried by anhydrous MgSO4 before being removed under vacuum. 1H NMR and 13C NMR were used to verified the structure of RCC3 (Figs. S3–S4). 1H NMR (CDCl3, 400 MHz): δ 7.12 (s, 12H, ArH), 3.81 (d, 12H, ArCH2), 3.58 (d, 12H, ArCH2), 2.21 (m, 12H, CH on cyclohexane), 2.02–0.98 (m, 48H, CH2 on cyclohexane) ppm. 13C NMR (CDCl3, 100 MHz): δ 141.29, 125.02, 61.34, 50.67, 31.73, 24.99 ppm.

CDs was synthesized according to previous report [43]. Briefly, citric acid powder (98%, anhydrous) (50 g) was put into a ceramic crucible and was thermolized at 180 °C for 40 h under air. After completion of the reaction, carboxylic acid capped CDs was produced. The CDs was dissolved in water by ultrasonic and freeze-dried to yield CDs as a yellow-orange powder. Elemental analysis reveals that CDs contains C (51.23%), H (3.67%) and N (0.00%).

CDs (1 g) was heated with neat thionyl chloride (30 mL) at 80 °C under a N2 atmosphere for 3 h. The excess thionyl chloride was evaporated using N2 as carrier gas. Then the CD-COCl was dissolved in dry tetrahydrofuran (5 mL) and filtered by 0.22 μm membrane filters. After removing solvent under reduced pressure, the CD-COCl (0.86 g) was obtained. Elemental analysis reveals that CD-COCl contains C (50.92%), H (2.32%) and N (0.00%).

RCC3 (100 mg, 0.09 mmol) was dissolved in 40 mL of dry tetrahydrofuran (THF) at room temperature in a 3-neck round bottom flask equipped with a dropping funnel. 256 μL triethylamine was added in to the solution of RCC3 and stirred at ice-bath for 15 min under N2 atmosphere. CD-COCl (50 mg) was dissolved in 20 mL of dry THF and then added drop wise manner with constant stirring. The resulting solution was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. After this period of time, the CD@RCC3 was separated by centrifugation, washed several times with ethanol and water, respectively. Elemental analysis reveals that CD@RCC3 contains C (56.42%), H (7.11%) and N (9.60%).

A quinine sulfate solution in 0.1 M H2SO4 (Quantum yield = 0.54) was chosen as standard [44]. The quantum yields (Q) of CD@RCC3 was calculated according to the following equation:
Q =
Q S
I
I S
A S
A
η
η S
Where Q is the quantum yield, I is the integrated intensity of the emission spectrum, A stands for the absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. In the equation, the subscript “S” stands for the standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedures for fluorescence sensing experiments
ce:section.ce:para: CD@RCC3 (12.5 mg) was added to methanol solutions (100 mL) to form suspension after ultrasound 15 min. The fluorescent measurements were performed by adding guest molecules (nitrophenol isomers or chiral alcohols) into CD@RCC3 suspension (1 mL). The mixtures were evenly dispersed after stirring for 3 min, then were measured under an excitation wavelength of 352 nm.
Exactly 3 mL of nitrophenol isomers or chiral alcohols methanol solutions was added to a quartz cuvette. The UV-is spectrum was recorded over the wavelength range of 200–650 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedures for lifetime decay experiments
ce:section.ce:para: Exactly 1 mL of CD@RCC3 suspension was added to a quartz cuvette. The emission decay at λ = 438 nm was recorded under excitation of 352 nm light. The experiment was repeated by adding guest molecules (nitrophenol isomers or chiral alcohols) into CD@RCC3 suspension (1 mL).

XPS measurements were further performed to investigate the specific bonding states of CD@RCC3. The survey spectrum of CD@RCC3 (Fig. 2c-1) shows three typical peaks corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s locating at 283.5, 397.5 and 531.2 eV, respectively. While only C 1s and O 1s are detected for CDs (Fig. 2c–2), indicating that N-containing groups have been integrated into the CDs. The C 1s spectra of the CD@RCC3 (Fig. 2d-1) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.8, 285.4, 286.3, and 288.2 eV, which can be attributed to CC, CO, CN, CO, respectively. Compared with the C 1s XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2d–2), a new peak at 286.3 eV assigned to CN is obviously observed in CD@RCC3 hybrid, indicating that CDs are chemically bound to RCC3. CD@RCC3 shows less peaks of carbon bound to oxygen than that of CDs (Fig. 2e), which may be caused by the chemical bonding between the C-atoms in the CDs and the N-atoms in the RCC3. The N 1s spectra of CD@RCC3 (Fig. 2f) exhibits two peaks located at 398.8 and 401.2 eV, which are responsible for the CN and NH, respectively. These results further confirm that CDs have been incorporated successfully with RCC3 via forming amide bond.

The TEM image (Fig. 3a) shows that CD@RCC3 is narrowly distributed without apparent aggregation. The size distribution of the CD@RCC3 ranged from 2.3 to 5.7 nm with an average diameter of 4.1 nm, which is obviously larger than that of the bare CDs with an average diameter of 3.2 nm (Fig. S5a). Fig. 3b shows the Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) image of CD@RCC3. As measured from the height profile, the average height of the CD@RCC3 is about 6.1 nm.

The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs and CD@RCC3 in methanol solution are shown in Fig. 4a and 4b. The absorption at 240 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [45], another absorption at 340 nm is ascribed to the n-π* transition of CDs [46]. As expected, the as-prepared CD@RCC3 was found highly fluorescent, both CDs and CD@RCC3 exhibits excitation-depended photoluminescence (PL) behaviour, and the increase of excitation wavelength leads to red-shifts in PL emission, which was one of the most special properties of CD materials. For CD@RCC3, strong fluorescence emissions were observed upon the excitation between 352 nm and 402 nm, with a maximum emission of about 438 nm at an excitation wavelength of 352 nm. Strong PL could be seen with naked eyes under UV light irradiation (Figs. 4a-b: inset), the photograph of CD under UV light (365 nm) was shown in light green, while CD@RCC3 in light blue. The quantum yield (Q) of CD@RCC3 was calculated to be 6.1% by utilizing quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as standard, which was higher than that of CDs (Q = 3%). The possible reason was that the surface of the CDs was passivated by RCC3, which resulted in reduction of the nonradiative decay [47]. We found that the fluorescence intensity of CD@RCC3 could remain stable under UV light irradiation (Fig. S5b), indicating its good anti-photobleaching. CD@RCC3 showed good dispersities in organic solvents (methanol; dichloromethane; DMF; trichloromethane; acetone; Fig. S5d) after ultrasonic treatment. Circular dichroism spectrum shows that CD@RCC3 processes chiral property (Fig. S5c).

Nitrophenol (NP) is a family of nitroaromatic compounds with three isomers, including o-nitrophenol (ONP), m-nitrophenol (MNP) and p-nitrophenol (PNP). Discrimination among NP isomers is particular importance. The application of CD@RCC3 in fluorescent sensing was first demonstrated by discriminating NP isomers. Although ONP, MNP and PNP have similarity in structure and chemical properties, we found that the fluorescent responses of CD@RCC3 to the three isomers were quite different. Interestingly, additions of NP to CD@RCC3 also led to different degrees of fluorescence quenching and red-shift in the emission band (Figs. 5a-b ). The fluorescence intensity of CD@RCC3 decreased about 63% with the addition of 20 μL ONP, accompanied by a 53 nm red-shift. With the addition of 20 μL PNP, 73% of quenching efficiency and a 45 nm red-shift in fluorescence emission spectra was observed. However, the same concentration of MNP only caused 42% decrement in fluorescence intensity, and had no obvious effect on emission wavelength. The experiment of discrimination NP isomers by carbon dots was also studied for comparison under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. S6, the quenching efficiency of ONP, MNP and PNP (31%, 17%, 40%, respectively) was lower than those obtained on CD@RCC3. Meanwhile, red-shift in emission wavelength could not be observed. The above results fully demonstrate that the promising potential of CD@RCC3 in fluorescence discrimination of NP isomers.

The Stern-Volmer equation was used to study the quenching behaviour of NP isomers on the CD@RCC3. As shown in Fig. S7, a good linear Stern-Volmer relationship was observed in CD@RCC3 for each analyte. Stern-Volmer quenching constants (K SV) were calculated by analysing the Stern-Volmer plots. For NP isomers incorporated CD@RCC3, K SV [ONP], K SV [MNP] and K SV [PNP] were 75, 24 and 95, respectively. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching degree of CD@RCC3 solutions by addition of NP isomers solutions followed the order of PNP > ONP > MNP.

As shown in Fig. S8, the absorption spectra of the NP isomers have different degrees of overlap with the fluorescence excitation of CD@RCC3, indicating the inner-filter effect is one of the factors causing the apparent fluorescence quenching and red-shift. We also found that additions of NP isomers into CD@RCC3 caused aggregation of CD@RCC3 (Fig. S9), which indicates there are strong interactions between CD@RCC3 and NP isomers. In order to gain a further valuable insight into the mode of the interaction between CD@RCC3 and nitrophenol, the fluorescence lifetime of CD@RCC3 before and after the addition of NP isomers was studied (Fig. S10), which suggested that non-fluorescent complexes were formed between CD@RCC3 and NP isomers. Since almost no overlap was observed between the absorption spectra of NP isomers and the emission spectrum of CD@RCC3 (Fig. S8), indicating there is no energy transfer process between NP isomers and CD@RCC3. Therefore, the interactions between CD@RCC3 and NP isomers possible is another factor affecting the fluorescence property of CD@RCC3. The possible mechanism is NP isomers are acidic in nature due to the electron-withdrawing and conjugation effect of nitro group, thereby leading to their proneness to form a strong ion pair of complexes with CD@RCC3 via their acidic OH proton bonded to the basic amino group of CD@RCC3 [36]. The more π-conjugated and rigidity of the formed complexes caused a decrease in the energy gap between the π and π* molecular orbitals which in turn resulted in a quenched and red-shifted peak in the fluorescence spectra of the CD@RCC3. The NP isomers show different degrees of acidity due to the different group position of nitro, the acidity of ONP (pK = 7.21) is very similar to that of PNP (pK = 7.16), while MNP shows higher basicity [48,49]. The acidity degree of NP isomers shows the same order of their fluorescence quenching degree, indicating that the difference in fluorescence quenching among NP isomers probably related to the different acidity degrees.

To further explore its application, we used CD@RCC3 to enantiorecognition of chiral alcohols, and D-/L-phenylalaninol (PA) and R-/S-phenylethanol (PE) were selected to target analytes. As shown in Fig. 6 , with additions of D-/L-phenylalaninol and R-/S-phenylethanol into CD@RCC3, a dramatic decrease in intensity at 438 nm was observed. From the slope of the Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. 6c), the K SV values for D-PA and L-PA were calculated as 1217 ± 79 M−1 and 2561 ± 152 M−1, respectively. The enantioselectivity factor K SV(L-PA)/K SV(D-PA) was determined to be 2.10 ± 0.18. The K SV values for R-PE and S-PE were 1723 ± 75 M−1 and 3371 ± 94 M−1, and the enantioselectivity factor K SV(S-PE)/K SV(R-PE) was 1.96 ± 0.15 (Fig. 6f). The above results demonstrate that CD@RCC3 might be a promising candidate for rapid differentiation of chiral compounds. However, bare CDs could not discriminate the two enantiomers (Fig. S11), which fully demonstrate the important role of RCC3 in the chiral discrimination. The above results demonstrate that CD@RCC3 is a promising candidate for recognition of chiral compounds.

The possible fluorescence enantiorecognition mechanism of CD@RCC3 towards chiral alcohols was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence lifetime of CD@RCC3 remained unchanged before and after the additions of D-/L-PA and R-/S-PE enantiomers, which suggests that the sensing processes belong to static quenching. The UV–vis spectra of the chiral alcohols and the emission spectrum did not have any overlap (Fig. S13), which rules out an energy transfer process. Thus, we speculate that the differentiation ability of CD@RCC3 results from the chiral-interactions between the chiral sites of CD@RCC3 and chiral alcohols. It was reported that chiral NH groups play an important role in the chiral recognition [50,51]. CD@RCC3 contains numerous NH active sites and chiral pocket that might be able to binding with high affinity to chiral molecules to form a chiral microenvironment. In addition, the hydroxyl group in the chiral alcohol interacting with the bare nitrogen atom and secondary amine moieties in CD@RCC3 might also play important roles in enantiomers discrimination [33,34].

In conclusion, we have synthesized a new fluorescent material (CD@RCC3) through post-synthetic modification of chiral porous organic cages (POCs) for fluorescent sensing. The CD@RCC3 possesses a strong and stable fluorescent property, and exhibits chiral property. It was exploited as fluorescence chemosensor to sensing of nitrophenol isomers, phenylalaninol and phenylethanol enantiomers. The results showed that CD@RCC3 was able to readily discriminate structural and chiral isomers. Both the inner-filter effect and intermolecular interactions led to decreases in fluorescence intensity when addition of NP isomers into CD@RCC3, while it seems that the discrimination chiral alcohols is based on the intermolecular chiral-chiral interactions. Our work described a novel material for fluorescence sensing, and provided a simple method for rapid discrimination of isomers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21675178, 21605163, 21475153 and 21775167), the Guangdong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2016A030313358), and the Special Funds for Public Welfare Research and Capacity Building in Guangdong Province of China (No. 2015A030401036), the Guangzhou Science and Technology Program of China (No. 201604020165), respectively.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.11.006.
